stories.headline: Nuggets defeat Timberwolves 116-105 in playoff series opener
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets shook off a sluggish start to beat the Minnesota Timberwolves 116-105 on Saturday in Game 1 of their first-round NBA playoff series. The win gives Denver a 1-0 series lead heading into Game 2.

The Nuggets, led by two-time MVP Nikola Jokic and bolstered by midseason acquisition Cam Johnson, were considered one of the stronger contenders in the Western Conference entering the postseason. Johnson, acquired last summer in a trade that sent Michael Porter Jr. to Brooklyn, has been widely regarded as an intelligent, versatile wing who complements Jokic's playmaking.

Denver's victory came on the same afternoon that Empower Field hosted a sold-out Inter Miami match featuring Lionel Messi, making for one of the most sports-saturated Saturdays in recent Denver memory. The Nuggets' next game in the series is scheduled for Monday.

stories.headline: Messi, Inter Miami top Colorado Rapids 3-2 at packed Empower Field
stories.summary:
Lionel Messi made his Colorado debut Saturday as Inter Miami defeated the Colorado Rapids 3-2 before a crowd of 75,824 at Empower Field at Mile High, one of the largest audiences for a soccer match in Denver history. The attendance figure was especially striking given the game fell on the same afternoon as a Nuggets playoff opener and a Rockies homestand.

Messi, 38, drew an electric response from fans throughout the match, including a wave of field-stormers in the final minutes as the Argentine superstar left the pitch. Argentine natives living along the Front Range turned out in force, with several telling reporters that Messi occupies a cultural status in Argentina comparable to the greatest figures across all American sports combined.

The match marked the Rapids' 30th anniversary celebration and was billed as a historic occasion for Major League Soccer in Colorado. Messi, who has won the Ballon d'Or eight times, is expected to retire following this summer, adding further significance to the appearance.

stories.headline: Avalanche's unconventional two-goalie playoff rotation draws scrutiny ahead of Kings series
stories.summary:
As the Colorado Avalanche prepare to open their first-round Stanley Cup playoff series against the Los Angeles Kings, coach Jared Bednar has indicated he intends to continue the two-goalie rotation that defined Colorado's regular season, a strategy historically associated with injury necessity rather than deliberate choice. Scott Wedgewood and Mackenzie Blackwood shared duties all year, combining to win the William Jennings Trophy for fewest goals allowed in the NHL.

Wedgewood, who posted a league-leading .921 save percentage and set career highs in starts and wins, is expected to start Game 1 on Sunday, though Bednar has not officially announced a starter. Blackwood, widely considered the higher-ceiling option entering the season, has been less consistent of late.

Bednar argued the rotation is sustainable precisely because both goaltenders have operated within it all season and are mentally prepared for the format. Critics note that virtually every modern Stanley Cup champion has settled on a clear No. 1 goaltender deep into the playoffs, making Denver's approach a notable gamble with championship implications.

stories.headline: Denver police mistakenly send citywide 'all clear' alert after armed robbery arrest in southwest Denver
stories.summary:
Denver police issued a shelter-in-place alert Saturday afternoon for a one-block area near West Gunnison Drive and South Zuni Street after an individual was robbed at gunpoint and two suspects fled into a nearby residence. Officers arrested both suspects, prompting an all-clear notification — but police mistakenly broadcast the all-clear to every Denver resident rather than limiting it to the affected neighborhood.

Department officials said they are reviewing how the error occurred. The incident marks the second time Denver police have sent a localized emergency alert to the entire city by mistake. A similar error occurred Jan. 17, when a barricaded-suspect alert intended for a two-block radius was transmitted citywide.

The repeated errors raise questions about the city's emergency notification systems and protocols for geofencing alerts. No injuries were reported in connection with Saturday's robbery.

stories.headline: Search intensifies for missing 27-year-old hunter on Mount Shavano in southern Colorado
stories.summary:
Eighty search and rescue personnel, along with dog teams, drones, helicopters, fixed-wing aircraft and mule teams, are combing the slopes of Mount Shavano in Chaffee County for Kaden Sites, a 27-year-old Salida man who went turkey hunting Wednesday afternoon and never returned. Sites left for the Blanks Cabin Trailhead at 1:30 p.m. Wednesday and was expected back in town by 3:45 p.m. for an appointment. His family found his truck at the trailhead with his phone inside, battery drained.

Sites is described as white, 6-foot-2 and 160 pounds with light brown hair, a goatee and mustache. He was last seen wearing a green hoodie, ripped khaki pants and hiking boots and was carrying a shotgun and binoculars. He may also have been wearing a ghillie camouflage suit, which could complicate aerial searches.

Chaffee County Sheriff Andy Rohrich asked the public to stay away from the search area after independent volunteers arrived Friday and impeded professional rescue crews. The search entered its fourth day Saturday with no confirmed sightings of Sites.

stories.headline: Panel recommends partial demolition of Denver Pavilions, residential towers on parking lots
stories.summary:
An advisory panel convened to study the future of Denver Pavilions, the city-owned open-air mall on the 16th Street Mall, has recommended demolishing the west side of the structure to create a publicly accessible park and partnering with private developers to build two residential towers on the parking lots behind the mall. Panel chair Nolan Marshall III, CEO of The Social District in Downtown Los Angeles, said the bold recommendations reflect a belief that Denver can undertake a transformative downtown project.

The panel's vision aligns with the recently adopted Downtown Area Plan, which contemplated replacing portions of the Pavilions with open space. Denver Pavilions has struggled with retail vacancies and shifting foot traffic patterns in the years since the pandemic reshaped downtown commerce.

No timeline or cost estimates were released alongside the recommendations, which must still be considered by city officials before any action is taken. The proposal represents one of the more dramatic potential changes to the downtown Denver streetscape in recent years.

stories.headline: $103.8 million apartment project breaks ground in RiNo after pandemic-era pivot away from office and hotel plans
stories.summary:
Invest Development Partners, along with New York-based firms Halpern Real Estate Ventures and RXR and Pennsylvania-based Korman Communities, held a groundbreaking Thursday for Ave Station House, a 13-story, 301-unit apartment building at 1485 40th St. in Denver's RiNo neighborhood. The $103.8 million project replaces plans for a 14-story office building and 16-story hotel that were shelved when the pandemic upended commercial real estate demand.

The development sits on 1.2 acres adjacent to RTD's 38th and Blake light rail station and will include 6,000 square feet of ground-floor retail. It is RXR's first Denver project. The building was scaled back from an earlier 17-story, roughly 400-unit design after lenders expressed reluctance to finance the larger structure.

Mayor Mike Johnston and Community Planning and Development Director Brad Buchanan both spoke at Thursday's ceremony. Buchanan, who recently took over the department, publicly offered his cell phone number to developers in attendance and pledged to make permitting more responsive. The project is expected to add significant residential density near one of Denver's busiest transit corridors.

stories.headline: Bipartisan effort on Colorado legislative caucus transparency collapses; liberal Democrats introduce rival bill
stories.summary:
An attempt to broker a transparency agreement between moderate and progressive Democrats in the Colorado legislature has broken down, with the more liberal faction introducing a new bill Friday without their moderate counterparts. The rift stems from a months-long dispute over the Colorado Opportunity Caucus, a moderate Democratic group that drew scrutiny after holding a retreat at a Vail hotel last October and declining to fully disclose its donors — including a nonprofit linked to Democratic primary challenges against progressive incumbents.

The original bipartisan measure, Senate Bill 108, would have required legislative caucuses to file monthly public financial reports with nonpartisan staff. Sponsored jointly by moderate Sen. Lindsey Daugherty of Arvada and progressive Sen. Mike Weissman of Aurora, the bill has sat without a committee hearing since February. Weissman and Rep. Yara Zokaie of Fort Collins then introduced Senate Bill 168, a nearly identical measure with technical drafting changes, without Daugherty or other moderate co-sponsors.

Daugherty accused the progressive faction of seeking selective transparency, while Weissman said the new bill simply resolved drafting ambiguities to ensure it targets only legislative caucuses rather than unrelated nonprofits. The episode highlights deepening tensions within the state's Democratic majority as the legislature heads toward the end of its session.

stories.headline: Elitch Gardens opens 136th season with patchy snow on the ground, summer festival lineup announced
stories.summary:
Elitch Gardens opened its 136th season Saturday morning in Denver despite temperatures in the low-to-mid 40s and patches of snow remaining from Friday's storm. Season pass holders and general admission guests lined up for more than an hour before the 10:30 a.m. opening, with a few dozen people in queue at the gate.

The park announced several festival-style events planned for the summer, including 'Taste of Elitch,' a food and drink festival scheduled for May 16-17 featuring seven cuisine zones — among them Asian barbecue, Cajun and Mediterranean offerings — available via tasting cards priced at $35 with park admission. Two additional festivals are planned for June and September, with details to be announced later.

Park officials also said a drone show is being planned to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the United States and the 150th anniversary of Colorado statehood. The Elitch waterpark is set to open Memorial Day weekend.

stories.headline: Critical fire weather conditions forecast for Denver metro and Front Range by midweek
stories.summary:
A period of warm, sunny weather is expected to give way to potentially dangerous fire conditions by Wednesday, according to National Weather Service forecasts. After temperatures climb into the 80s on the plains Monday and Tuesday with light winds, Wednesday is expected to bring gusty southwest winds, humidity dropping below 10 percent and continued heat — a combination forecasters say will likely produce critical fire weather across much of the Denver metro area and Front Range.

The warm-up follows last week's spring snowstorm, which deposited as much as six inches in Roxborough Park, 3.5 inches at Denver International Airport and varying totals across the metro. Saturday's sunshine rapidly melted most of that accumulation.

Beginning Thursday, a series of Pacific storm systems is expected to bring increasing chances of rain and mountain snow, potentially delivering measurable moisture before the end of next week. The transition from extreme dryness to unsettled conditions means the window of elevated fire danger is likely concentrated around Wednesday.

stories.headline: USWNT closes Japan friendly series with 3-0 win at Dick's Sporting Goods Park; Colorado's Lindsey Heaps draws loud ovation
stories.summary:
The U.S. Women's National Team concluded a three-game friendly series against Japan with a 3-0 victory Friday night at Dick's Sporting Goods Park in Commerce City, finishing the series 2-1. Nearly 18,000 fans attended, with a notable contingent traveling from out of state, including fans from New Jersey, El Paso and as far as Australia.

Colorado native Lindsey Heaps, who recently signed a pre-contract to join the new Denver Summit FC for the upcoming NWSL season, received the loudest individual ovation of the night when lineups were announced and again when she was substituted off in the 63rd minute. Heaps, who plays for French powerhouse OL Lyonnes and scored her 40th international goal earlier in the series, acknowledged the emotional weight of playing in front of nearly 50 friends and family members.

Windsor native Sophia Wilson, a forward on the national team, praised Denver as a women's sports city and expressed hope that the arrival of Summit FC — which drew an NWSL-record 63,004 fans for its inaugural home match — would sustain and grow the area's soccer fanbase. Local businesses near the stadium reported a boost in activity tied to the match.

weather.notable: About 11 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Talking about music is like dancing about architecture.

topStory.headline: Trump Supporters Back Iran War, Tell Pope Leo to 'Stay in His Lane' as Pontiff Denies Seeking Debate With President
topStory.summary:
Pope Leo XIV and President Donald Trump remain locked in a public dispute over the U.S. military campaign against Iran, even as the pontiff sought Saturday to soften perceptions of the clash. Speaking to reporters aboard the papal plane during his 11-day African tour, Leo said it was "not in my interest at all" to debate Trump, and explained that remarks he delivered in Cameroon on Thursday — in which he denounced "tyrants" and war spending — had been written weeks before Trump began attacking him publicly.

Trump has targeted Leo repeatedly on Truth Social and in public statements, calling the Chicago-born pontiff "WEAK on crime" and "terrible on foreign policy," and accusing him — falsely, according to reporting — of wanting a nuclear-armed Iran. Leo has countered that Trump's threat to "annihilate Iranian civilization" was "truly unacceptable" and said he has "no fear of the Trump administration." Vice President JD Vance, a 2019 Catholic convert, also weighed in, saying Leo should "be careful" when speaking on matters of policy and invoking the theological concept of "just war theory" to suggest the military action could be morally justified.

Despite the high-profile clash between the leader of 1.4 billion Catholics and a president who has cultivated strong support among Christian conservatives, interviews with more than 20 Trump supporters at events in Las Vegas and Phoenix revealed little sign of divided loyalties. Catholic Trump backers repeatedly said the pope was overstepping his role, with one attendee telling NBC News the pontiff should "stay in your lane." Many supporters said Trump was right to use military force against Iran and within his authority to rebuff the pope's criticism.

The dispute has been further inflamed by a separate controversy in which Trump posted an AI-generated image of himself depicted as Jesus healing a sick man on Truth Social. Trump later deleted the image and claimed he believed it showed him as a Red Cross doctor. The episode drew additional criticism from Christian communities already unsettled by the administration's religious rhetoric, including Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's call for Americans to pray for U.S. military victory in Iran "in the name of Jesus Christ."

The standoff marks an unusual public rupture between the White House and the Vatican, with no signs of imminent resolution. Leo has vowed to continue speaking out for peace and dialogue, while Trump and his allies show no indication of softening their posture on Iran or their criticism of the pope. How the confrontation affects Trump's coalition of Christian conservatives and "America First" voters — two groups whose priorities do not always align — remains an open political question as the Iran conflict continues.
